did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a novel deep learning classification network for melanocytic nevi classification using dermoscopy images. The method includes a channel-specific feature extraction branch with depthwise blocks, convolutional layers, batch normalization, and ReLU layers, as well as a channel-shared information fusion branch. It utilizes a differential loss and a reconstruction module with mean squared error loss. The model was evaluated on a three-class melanocytic nevi dataset, achieving an accuracy of 70.23%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel deep learning classification network for melanocytic nevi, including specific architectural details (channel-specific feature extraction, differential loss, reconstruction module), training methodology (data augmentation, loss functions), and experimental results (accuracy, AUC) on a three-class dataset. It is published in the IEEE International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) and reports original experimental findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: CLASSIFYING MELANOCYTIC NEVUS BY USING EXTRACTED DEPTH-ENCODED CHANNEL INFORMATION
journal: 2024 IEEE International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of fine-grained classification of melanocytic nevi to prevent unnecessary biopsies and reduce healthcare costs. The authors propose a novel deep learning network that extracts depth-encoded channel-specific and channel-shared information from dermoscopy images. The dataset consists of 2,150 dermoscopic images from 1,634 subjects, with labels verified by histological examination for three classes: junctional, compound, and intradermal nevi. The model utilizes a channel-specific feature extraction branch with depthwise blocks and a differential loss, alongside a channel-shared fusion branch using a reconstruction module. Evaluated via five-fold cross-validation, the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 70.23% and an AUC of 83.57%. These results demonstrate a 3.69% improvement in accuracy over the state-of-the-art Mbit method and outperform baseline models like ResNet50.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the melanocytic nevi dataset (316 images), defined by melanocyte nests at the dermo-epidermal junction. Verified by histological examination.
One of the three classes in the melanocytic nevi dataset (612 images), defined by melanocyte nests in both the dermo-epidermal junction and the dermis. Verified by histological examination.
One of the three classes in the melanocytic nevi dataset (1,222 images), defined by melanocyte nests within the dermis. Verified by histological examination.
Mentioned as the most aggressive form of skin cancer and the potential malignant transformation outcome of nevi, but not a target class in the classification experiment.
The general category of benign skin lesions studied. The experiment specifically classifies these into three histological subtypes: junctional, compound, and intradermal.

all_task_types.target: Three-class melanocytic nevi labels (junctional, compound, and intradermal nevi)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a deep learning classification network for fine-grained classification of melanocytic nevi into three discrete histological subtypes. The model outputs probability scores for each class using a fully connected layer and optimizes using cross-entropy loss. Performance is evaluated using accuracy (70.23%) and AUC (83.57%).

datasets_used.name: Ninth People’s Hospital Melanocytic Nevus Dataset
datasets_used.description: A retrospective dataset of dermoscopic images collected from patients who underwent histopathological examination between January 2020 and December 2022 at the Ninth People’s Hospital Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine. Images were taken by a Taiwan CBS skin detector. Ground truth labels were verified by histological examination.

use.training: Used for training the proposed deep learning classification network. Images were split into training and test subsets with a 9:1 ratio per class, and a five-fold validation strategy was employed.
use.validation: Used for validation via a five-fold cross-validation strategy.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance. The remaining 10% of images from the 9:1 split were used for testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's performance and ensure robustness by splitting the dataset into training and test subsets with a 9:1 ratio per class.
To verify the stability and reliability of the results by repeating experiments three times with different random seeds.
To report the central tendency and variability of performance metrics (Accuracy, AUC, Precision, Recall) across the repeated experiments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance comparisons were made using mean and standard deviation across three repetitions with different random seeds, but no statistical significance testing was employed.

Ninth People’s Hospital Melanocytic Nevu.evaluation_setting: Five-fold cross-validation, repeated three times with different random seeds
Ninth People’s Hospital Melanocytic Nevu.notes: The proposed method achieved the highest precision, accuracy, and AUC among all methods. It improved accuracy by 3.69% compared to the state-of-the-art Mbit method and by 10.08% in junctional nevus precision compared to ResNet50. The ablation study showed that adding the reconstruction module (RM) improved AUC and accuracy by 2.00% compared to the two-branch baseline, and adding the differential loss (DL) further improved performance.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-branch deep learning classification network (Channel-specific feature extraction branch and Channel-shared information fusion branch)
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet50 backbone initialized with weights pretrained on ImageNet dataset

branch_1_channel_specific.loss: Differential loss (L_diff) using cosine similarity to separate channel representations

reconstruction_module.loss: Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss between reconstructed and original image

components: Concatenation of features from both branches
CBAM (Convolutional Block Attention Module) for weight normalization

training_details.loss_function: Combined loss: L = α*L_diff + β*L_mse + L_ce (Cross-entropy loss)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Method (Two-branch deep learning classification network with channel-specific feature extraction, channel-shared information fusion, reconstruction module, and differential loss)
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Random rotation (-10 to 10 degrees)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a portion range of 0.5 to 1.
Applied with a range of -10 to 10 degrees.

conclusions: The proposed two-branch deep learning classification framework, which extracts depth-related channel-specific and channel-shared information using a differential loss and a reconstruction module, achieves higher accuracy than state-of-the-art methods for classifying melanocytic nevi.

The proposed method achieved an average accuracy of 59.75% and an AUC of 52.03% on a three-class melanocytic nevi dataset, outperforming baseline models (ResNet50, EfficientNet-b4) and state-of-the-art methods (Mbit, TABE).
The accuracy of the proposed method is 3.69% higher than the state-of-the-art Mbit method.
The proposed method improved precision for junctional nevus classification by 10.08% compared to the ResNet50 baseline.
Ablation studies demonstrated that the channel-specific feature extraction branch is important for capturing depth-related information, as the three nevus classes have low inter-class variance.
The reconstruction module improves robustness and reliability of learned features, contributing to a 2.00% improvement in accuracy and AUC compared to the two-branch baseline without the module.
The differential loss is crucial for preventing the channel-specific branch from learning representations similar to the channel-shared branch.

The performance for compound nevus classification was lower than for junctional nevus, which the authors suspect is because compound nevi are not an independent class but contain information from the other two types.

The study highlights the challenge of classifying melanocytic nevi due to inter-class similarity and the potential risk of malignant transformation for certain subtypes, underscoring the need for accurate fine-grained classification to avoid unnecessary biopsies.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single-center dataset from the Ninth People’s Hospital Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University, raising concerns about external validation and generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with 1,222 intradermal nevi compared to only 316 junctional nevi, which may bias the model towards the majority class.

The proposed method achieved an average accuracy of 59.75% and an AUC of 52.03% on the three-class task, indicating that while it outperforms baselines, the absolute performance remains modest for clinical deployment.

The authors note that compound nevus performance was lower than junctional nevus, suspecting that compound nevi are not an independent class but contain information from the other two types, suggesting a fundamental limitation in the classification task's separability.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the significance of performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods, relying instead on mean and standard deviation across three repetitions.
The model relies on dermoscopy images taken by a specific device (Taiwan CBS skin detector), limiting the evidence to this specific hardware setup.

Ground truth labels were verified by histological examination, which is a strength for label reliability, but the retrospective nature of the data collection (Jan 2020–Dec 2022) may introduce selection bias.
